symptoms.symptom_name: Severe worst-ever generalised headache, rapidly worsening over 12 hours

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Severe progressive headache is a cardinal feature of the meningitis tetrad.
Worst-ever headache also fits SAH — but the gradual onset and fever argue against a thunderclap bleed.
Severe headache can be migrainous but does not explain fever or meningism.
Fever with headache and neck stiffness strongly raises meningeal infection.
SAH is typically afebrile at onset — a high fever points away from a primary bleed.
Migraine does not cause a high fever.
Photophobia reflects meningeal irritation; part of the classic constellation.

symptoms.symptom_name: Subjective severe neck stiffness (cannot touch chin to chest)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Patient-reported neck stiffness limiting flexion is a key meningism feature elicitable by history in telehealth.

symptoms.symptom_name: New altered mental status / confusion / drowsiness

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: New confusion signals cerebral involvement (meningoencephalitis) and worsening severity — a red flag mandating emergency escalation.
Encephalitic features overlap; does not change the urgent escalation.
Non-specific, but consistent with raised intracranial pressure and the overall picture.

symptoms.symptom_name: Absence of a non-blanching petechial/purpuric rash at present

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A currently-absent rash does NOT exclude meningococcal disease — the rash is often late; continued monitoring is required.

symptoms.symptom_name: Nuchal rigidity on passive neck flexion

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Objective meningeal sign — sealed clinician examination; the AI Doctor infers meningism from reported stiffness, not a passive-flexion exam.
Classic meningeal sign — cannot be elicited via telehealth; sealed answer key.
Classic meningeal sign — cannot be elicited via telehealth; sealed answer key.

symptoms.symptom_name: Measured vitals: temperature 39.4 C, HR 125, BP 100/60, RR 22, SpO2 98%

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Objective fever and sepsis physiology (tachycardia, low-normal BP) — clinician-measured, sealed; the AI infers fever from lay report, not a measured value.

symptoms.symptom_name: CSF pleocytosis with elevated protein and low glucose on lumbar puncture

investigation_details.expected_result: Neutrophil-predominant pleocytosis, elevated CSF protein, low CSF glucose — consistent with bacterial meningitis
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: Without CSF the causative organism and definitive diagnosis are unconfirmed — but antibiotics must NOT be delayed for it.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Bacterial CSF profile confirms the diagnosis — hospital investigation, never available in telehealth in real time.
Objective depressed conscious level — sealed clinician measurement corroborating the lay report of confusion.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Severe headache + high fever + photophobia + neck stiffness together are highly specific for meningeal infection and mandate emergency escalation.

symptom_clusters.target_diagnosis: acute bacterial meningitis with early sepsis
symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: New altered mental status with tachycardia and fever indicates cerebral involvement and systemic sepsis — the highest-acuity picture.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Empirical IV antibiotics BEFORE lumbar puncture (do not delay)
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Time-critical: empirical ceftriaxone plus dexamethasone must be given on suspicion; delay increases death and disability.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: CT head prior to lumbar puncture (given altered mental status)
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Reduced conscious level warrants CT before LP to exclude a mass lesion / raised ICP — but must not delay antibiotics.
Confirms bacterial meningitis and guides organism-directed therapy; performed in hospital after antibiotics are started.
Identify the organism and quantify the sepsis response.
